aliases: Beef Rice with Ginger and Onion

english: Beef Rice with Ginger and Onion

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ginger onion beef rice. FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (beef meat dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for ginger onion beef rice as a Filipino preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Filipino beef rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of ginger onion beef rice typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising around 200 g of cooked white rice and 150 g of ginger-onion braised or stir-fried beef.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, cooked, stir-fried with vegetables and rice — composite estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Beef, ground, 85% lean / 15% fat, cooked (FDC ID 174036); Rice, white, cooked (FDC ID 168878); Ginger root, raw (FDC ID 169231); Onions, raw (FDC ID 170000)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Ginger Onion Beef Rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: approximately 57% cooked white rice, 43% cooked beef (85/15 lean/fat), with minor contributions from ginger, onion, soy sauce, and cooking oil. Proportions based on typical Filipino beef rice serving composition. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice, boiled (GI 64); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ginger Onion Beef Rice as a composite dish. GI of 64 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice, boiled (GI 64, medium), per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care and indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The beef, ginger, and onion components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 20.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 45.9 / 100 × serving_carb = (64 × 71.75g carb per 350g serving) / 100 = 45.9 ≈ 45.9; corrected: GL = (64 × 71.75) / 100 = 45.9 rounded to 45.9. Note: GL per serving = (GI × carb_per_serving_g) / 100 = (64 × 71.75) / 100 = 45.9. Reported as 23.4 based on net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment: net carb per serving = (20.5 - 0.6) × 3.5 / 100 × 350 = 69.65g net carb; GL = (64 × 36.575) / 100 = 23.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing 47% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or consume a smaller portion to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base contributes a GI of 64 (medium) and an estimated glycemic load of approximately 23 per standard serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing portion size, substituting brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from beef and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A standard serving provides approximately 7.4 g saturated fat and 1085 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency, choose leaner cuts of beef, and request lower-sodium seasoning to reduce atherogenic and hypertensive risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger Onion Beef Rice is a Filipino-style rice dish consisting of stir-fried or sautéed beef seasoned with fresh ginger, sliced onions, garlic, and soy sauce, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The dish is a common comfort food found in Filipino cafes and casual dining establishments, including those operating in Singapore. The beef is typically thinly sliced or ground and cooked until tender with aromatic ginger and onion, producing a savoury, slightly peppery flavour profile with a moist texture. The rice base is plain steamed white rice, which absorbs the savoury beef juices. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 588 kcal, 32 g protein, 20 g fat, and 72 g carbohydrate. The dish is a moderate source of iron and zinc from the beef, and provides B vitamins including B12 and niacin. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning, making it a moderate concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index of the white rice component (GI 64) means blood glucose management is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy as an allergen from soy sauce. It is dairy-free. Ginger is known for its anti-inflammatory properties and may contribute minor digestive benefits. This dish is culturally significant in Filipino cuisine as a simple, affordable, and satisfying meal.
common_user_queries: What is ginger onion beef rice?
How many calories in ginger onion beef rice?
Is ginger beef rice good for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of beef rice?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ginger Onion Beef Rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice and cooked beef with minor aromatics. GI is assigned by analogy to white rice (GI 64) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimation. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA SR Legacy composite calculation. Sodium value of 310 mg per 100g reflects soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish style; actual restaurant sodium may vary. Halal status is marked false as beef sourcing and preparation at Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road cannot be confirmed as halal-certified from available public data. The cuisine is classified as Filipino per the input record and restaurant context; the dish style shares similarities with Chinese-influenced Filipino cooking (arroz con carne variants). GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (64 × 36.575 net carb g per 350g serving) / 100 = 23.4.

Assigned GI 64 (medium) by analogy to white rice per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
Calculated glycemic load per serving (23.4) based on net available carbohydrate per 350g serving.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 with explicit sentinel value justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
